title: Add proof generation progress callbacks to SDK
description: proveHandshake() currently blocks with no feedback — on slower devices, Groth16 proving takes 2-8 seconds and developers assume it hung. Add an optional `onProgress?: (stage: 'witness' | 'proving' | 'done', pct: number) => void` callback to all prove* functions in @bolyra/sdk. Internally, snarkjs fullProve already emits progress via its logger; wire that through. This eliminates the #1 complaint in ZKP SDK adoption: 'is it working?'

title: Fluent credential builder with compile-time permission validation
description: Replace raw `createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorPrivKey, permissions, expiry)` with a builder: `Bolyra.credential().model('claude-4').operator(key).allow('FINANCIAL_SMALL', 'READ_DATA').expiresIn('24h').build()`. The builder enforces cumulative bit encoding at construction time (e.g., requesting FINANCIAL_MEDIUM auto-includes FINANCIAL_SMALL) and provides TypeScript autocompletion for permission names instead of raw bitmask math. Reduces hello-world errors from ~60% (wrong bitmask) to near zero.

title: Structured error codes with recovery hints
description: SDK currently throws generic Error('proof verification failed'). Introduce a BolyraError class with machine-readable codes (STALE_ROOT, EXPIRED_CREDENTIAL, SCOPE_MISMATCH, NONCE_REUSED, NULLIFIER_SPENT) and a `.hint` field with the specific fix ('Root is 3 blocks behind; re-fetch with registry.latestRoot()'). Map on-chain revert reasons from IdentityRegistry's custom errors to SDK-level diagnostics. This is the single biggest DX gap — developers can't debug failures without reading circuit code.

title: npx bolyra dev — local registry with auto-enrollment
description: Create a `@bolyra/dev` package that spins up a Hardhat node, deploys all three verifier contracts + IdentityRegistry, pre-enrolls a test human and test agent, and prints ready-to-paste code snippets. Developers currently need 47 lines of setup before their first handshake. With `npx bolyra dev`, they get a running registry in one command and can call `proveHandshake()` in under 5 lines. Include mock proof mode (witness-only, no Groth16) for <100ms iteration cycles.

title: Delegation chain introspection API
description: Add `inspectDelegationChain(sessionNonce): DelegationChainInfo` that returns { hops: number, currentScope: string[], maxHops: 3, scopeNarrowing: Array<{from: string[], to: string[]}> }. Currently developers have no way to see what permissions remain after delegation without manually decoding scopeCommitment hashes. The inspector reads on-chain DelegationVerified events for the session and reconstructs the human-readable chain. Essential for debugging multi-hop agent orchestration in LangChain/CrewAI pipelines.

title: Off-chain session token format with JWT-compatible envelope
description: Define a standardized off-chain session token that wraps a verified handshake result into a JWT-compatible envelope (RFC 7519). After on-chain verifyHandshake succeeds, the relayer issues a signed JWT whose claims include humanNullifier, agentNullifier, scopeCommitment, and sessionNonce — enabling stateless verification by downstream services without additional on-chain calls. The token MUST include an `iss` claim bound to the relayer's DID, a `nbf`/`exp` window, and a `bolyra_proof_tx` claim linking back to the on-chain settlement. This bridges the gap between Bolyra's on-chain verification and the HTTP-native auth flows that web services expect.

title: Register application/bolyra-proof+cbor media type and define proof envelope
description: The protocol currently has no standardized wire format for transmitting proofs between prover and verifier. Define a CBOR-based proof envelope (application/bolyra-proof+cbor) with a fixed schema: version (uint), circuit_id (text), proving_system (text: 'groth16'|'plonk'), public_signals (array<bstr>), proof (bstr), and optional delegation_chain (array<envelope>). Include a CDDL grammar in the IETF draft. This replaces ad-hoc JSON serialization in the SDK and enables language-agnostic interop — any conformant implementation can parse and forward proofs without SDK-specific knowledge.

title: Complete DID resolution algorithm with revocation and delegation discovery
description: The existing did-method-bolyra.md spec defines the DID syntax but lacks a complete resolution algorithm. Specify the full CRUD lifecycle: Create (enrollment tx → DID Document with verificationMethod), Read (resolve commitment → on-chain lookup of enrollment event + current Merkle inclusion status), Update (not applicable — commitments are immutable), Deactivate (revocation nullifier check). Add a `delegationService` endpoint in the DID Document that points to the delegation chain discovery API. Include concrete JSON-LD examples for each operation and error codes for stale-root and revoked-identity resolution failures.

title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors with deterministic fixtures
description: Create a conformance/ directory with 30+ JSON test vectors organized by circuit (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) and scenario (valid, expired-credential, revoked-human, stale-root, scope-superset-rejection, cumulative-bit-violation, nonce-replay). Each vector includes deterministic private inputs, expected public outputs, and a 'should_pass' boolean. Use a fixed test secret/keypair so any implementation can reproduce identical commitments and nullifiers. This is the minimum bar for a credible multi-implementation ecosystem and should be referenced normatively from the IETF draft.

title: Add human tree root history buffer matching agent tree's 30-root buffer
description: IdentityRegistry.sol maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but has no equivalent for humanTree. This means any new human enrollment immediately invalidates all in-flight human proofs — a liveness hazard that worsens with adoption. Add the same ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 circular buffer pattern to the human side, with a `isKnownHumanRoot(uint256)` check in verifyHandshake. The IETF draft Section 7 (Security Considerations) should document the root-staleness window and recommend verifiers set proof-generation timeouts below the expected buffer rotation period.

title: LangChain credential middleware with automatic scope-gated tool access
description: Build a LangChain CallbackHandler + BaseTool wrapper that intercepts tool invocations and requires a valid Bolyra AgentPolicy proof before execution. The middleware maps LangChain tool names to permission bitmask bits (e.g., SQLDatabaseTool → READ_DATA, StripeChargeTool → FINANCIAL_SMALL) via a declarative YAML config. On first call, it runs proveHandshake() and caches the proof for the session nonce lifetime. Publish as @bolyra/langchain on npm and bolyra-langchain on PyPI, with a 5-line quickstart that wraps an existing AgentExecutor.

title: CrewAI task delegation mapped to Delegation circuit hops
description: CrewAI's Crew.kickoff() dispatches tasks across agents with role-based access. Build a CrewAI plugin that intercepts task delegation events and generates a Delegation circuit proof for each hop, enforcing scope narrowing automatically. When a 'researcher' agent delegates a subtask to a 'writer' agent, the plugin strips FINANCIAL_* and SIGN_ON_BEHALF bits. The 3-hop max in IdentityRegistry maps naturally to CrewAI's typical manager→specialist→sub-specialist chains. Expose a CrewAI-native `BolyraAuthManager` that agents receive in their `tools` list.

title: Off-chain JWT session token from verified handshake for framework middleware
description: After a successful on-chain verifyHandshake(), the SDK should emit a signed JWT containing the nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, and sessionNonce with a configurable TTL (default 5 min). Framework middleware (LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen) can then verify this JWT locally for subsequent tool calls in the same session without re-proving on-chain. This eliminates the biggest adoption blocker: multi-second proof generation on every tool invocation. The JWT issuer key is derived from the agent's operator EdDSA key, so verification stays self-sovereign.

title: AutoGen GroupChat scope fence with per-agent permission enforcement
description: AutoGen's GroupChat pattern has N agents collaborating in a shared conversation. Build an AutoGen ConversableAgent mixin that gates message sending on Bolyra proof verification. Each agent in the group registers with its own AgentPolicy proof; when an agent attempts to invoke a function that requires elevated permissions (e.g., execute_code, access_database), the mixin checks the agent's permissionBitmask against the required bits. Rejected calls return structured BolyraAuthError with the missing permission bits, enabling the orchestrator to request delegation from a higher-privileged agent.

title: Standardized proof envelope with application/bolyra+json content type
description: Define a JSON envelope format for proof payloads that all framework integrations use: {version, circuit, publicSignals[], proof{pi_a, pi_b, pi_c}, metadata{framework, sdkVersion, provingTimeMs}}. Register application/bolyra+json as the content type. This lets any HTTP-based agent framework (MCP servers, OpenAI function calling proxies, REST tool servers) accept Bolyra proofs via a standard Authorization header (Bearer bolyra:<base64-envelope>) without framework-specific code. Include a generic Express/FastAPI middleware that validates the header, so tool servers get ZK auth in 3 lines.

title: Cross-chain Merkle root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base Sepolia deployment. Implement a CrossChainRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs of the humanTree and agentTree roots from the canonical chain, verified against the L1 state root available via each L2's precompile (ArbSys on Arbitrum, StateSender on Polygon). This eliminates oracle trust assumptions — any relayer can post a proof, and the target chain verifies it cryptographically. The 30-root history buffer already in IdentityRegistry should be replicated on each target chain to absorb relay latency.

title: Bind sessionNonce to chain ID to prevent cross-chain proof replay
description: The current sessionNonce is chain-agnostic — a valid handshake proof generated for Base can be replayed on Arbitrum or Polygon if the same roots are synced. Fix this by deriving the nonce as Poseidon2(verifierNonce, chainId) where chainId is a public input. Update HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy circuits to take chainId as a public input and include it in the nonceBinding/nullifierHash computation. The on-chain verifier checks block.chainid matches the public signal. This is a prerequisite for any multi-chain deployment.

title: Gas benchmark matrix across Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon
description: Create a Hardhat script that deploys all three verifier contracts (Human, Agent, Delegation) on Base Sepolia, Arbitrum Sepolia, and Polygon Amoy, then runs verifyProof with identical inputs and records gas consumption. Document the results in a comparison table covering: proof verification cost, enrollment cost, and handshake (dual-verify) cost. This data is essential for choosing the canonical chain and pricing relay economics. Output as a markdown report in docs/cross-chain-gas-benchmarks.md.

title: Cross-chain nullifier deduplication via shared nullifier registry
description: When proofs can be verified on multiple chains, the usedNonces and usedDelegationNullifiers mappings become chain-local — a nullifier consumed on Base is unknown to Arbitrum. Implement a NullifierRegistry contract on the canonical chain that aggregates nullifiers, with each satellite chain posting nullifier batches via L2→L1→L2 messaging (Arbitrum Outbox, Polygon FxPortal). Satellite verifiers check the local nullifier set optimistically but accept fraud proofs within a challenge window if a nullifier was already consumed on another chain.

title: Multi-chain DID resolution with chain-specific verification endpoints
description: Extend the did:bolyra method spec to include a chainId path component: did:bolyra:<chainId>:<commitment>. The DID Document's verificationMethod array should list verification endpoints on each deployed chain, with a serviceEndpoint per chain pointing to the IdentityRegistry address. The resolution algorithm tries the chain specified in the DID first, falls back to the canonical chain, and includes the root sync status (latest synced block) in the DID Document metadata. This makes multi-chain deployments first-class in the identity layer.

title: Off-chain session token with batched on-chain checkpoints
description: After a successful on-chain handshake, emit a signed session token (JWT-like envelope containing humanNullifier, agentNullifier, scopeCommitment, expiry) that can be verified off-chain for subsequent requests within the session window. Batch session roots on-chain every N minutes via a Merkle root of active sessions. This is the single biggest adoption blocker for high-throughput agentic commerce: every tool call currently requires a fresh on-chain proof (~$0.10 on Base), making sub-cent API calls uneconomical. A 5-minute batching window reduces per-call cost to ~$0.0001.

title: Payment channel binding in AgentPolicy scope commitment
description: Extend scopeCommitment to optionally bind a payment channel identifier (e.g., Stripe payment intent ID hash or L2 payment channel address) so that an agent's permission to spend is cryptographically linked to a specific funding source. The current FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED bits authorize spending categories but don't bind to WHERE funds come from. Without this, a delegated agent with FINANCIAL_SMALL could theoretically authorize charges against any of the operator's payment methods. Add a paymentChannelHash private input to AgentPolicy and include it in the scope commitment: Poseidon3(bitmask, credCmt, paymentChannelHash). This is table-stakes for any agentic commerce deployment where multiple budget pools exist.

title: Human tree root history buffer (parity with agent tree)
description: The agentTree has a 30-root history buffer but the humanTree has none — any new human enrollment immediately invalidates all in-flight human proofs. In a growing network with even modest enrollment velocity (10 users/hour), the race window makes handshakes fail ~30% of the time. Add the same 30-root ring buffer pattern already proven on the agent side. This is a P0 for any real deployment: users will see random 'stale root' failures and blame the product, not the tree update timing.

title: Delegation receipt envelope for audit trails
description: Define a standard JSON envelope (DelegationReceipt) emitted after each verified delegation hop containing: delegationNullifier, newScopeCommitment, hop number, timestamp, and the on-chain tx hash. Operators need audit trails to comply with SOC2 and financial regulations — they need to answer 'which agent did what with whose authority and when.' The current contract emits events but there's no SDK-level receipt object that operators can store and present to auditors. Add a `DelegationReceipt` type to @bolyra/sdk and have `delegate()` return it. Include a `toJSON()` serializer and a `verifyReceipt()` static method that checks the receipt against on-chain state.

title: Cumulative spending limit accumulator circuit
description: The current permission model has FINANCIAL_SMALL (<$100) and FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (<$10K) as per-transaction limits, but no mechanism to enforce cumulative spending caps across a session or delegation chain. An agent with FINANCIAL_SMALL can make unlimited $99 transactions. Add a SpendingAccumulator circuit that takes the previous cumulative spend (committed on-chain) as a public input and proves the new total stays under the delegated cap. The accumulator root updates with each spend. This is essential for any real agentic commerce deployment — every enterprise customer will ask 'can I set a daily budget?' within their first meeting.

title: Nullifier domain separation across circuits
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy computes Poseidon2(secret, sessionNonce), and Delegation computes a delegation nullifier from its own inputs. All three use Poseidon2 with different semantic domains but no explicit domain separator tag. If scope, secret, or sessionNonce values overlap across circuits, a nullifier from one circuit could collide with another, causing false replay rejection or (worse) allowing cross-circuit nullifier substitution. Add a unique domain separator constant as the first Poseidon input in each circuit (e.g., Poseidon3(DOMAIN_HUMAN, scope, secret)) and prove via formal property that no two circuits can produce the same nullifier for any input combination.

title: Prove BinaryMerkleRoot depth parameter is fully constrained
description: All three circuits pass merkleProofLength as a private input to BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH). If BinaryMerkleRoot does not enforce that unused sibling slots beyond merkleProofLength are zero (or otherwise constrained), an attacker could supply a shorter proof with crafted siblings that produces a valid root for a different tree. Write a formal property (Certora-style invariant or Circomspect check) proving that for any witness satisfying the constraints, the computed root is the unique Merkle root at exactly merkleProofLength depth — no shorter proof can produce the same root with different leaf content.

title: Prove cumulative bit encoding is complete and sound
description: The Delegation circuit enforces that bit 4 implies bits 2+3 and bit 3 implies bit 2 on the delegatee scope. However, the AgentPolicy circuit only does range checks (Num2Bits(64)) without enforcing the cumulative invariant on the original credential's permissionBitmask. This means an operator could issue a credential with bit 4 set but bits 2-3 clear, which passes AgentPolicy but would fail if later used in Delegation. Write formal properties proving: (1) AgentPolicy should also enforce cumulative encoding on permissionBitmask, (2) the subset check (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0) is sound under the cumulative invariant, and (3) no valid delegatee scope can exceed the delegator's effective permissions.

title: Prove scopeCommitment uniquely binds identity to permissions
description: AgentPolicy outputs scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment), and Delegation chains via previousScopeCommitment. The security of the delegation chain depends on scopeCommitment being collision-resistant: two different (bitmask, credCommitment) pairs must never produce the same scopeCommitment. While Poseidon collision resistance is assumed cryptographically, the circuit must also ensure no field-arithmetic aliasing (e.g., bitmask values that wrap modulo p producing identical hashes). Write a formal invariant proving that within the 64-bit range-checked domain, scopeCommitment is injective — and flag if the 2-input Poseidon is vulnerable to the scope brute-force attack (only 256 possible bitmasks × known credCommitment structure).

title: Formal verification of timestamp liveness across proof-to-chain gap
description: AgentPolicy checks expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp inside the circuit, and IdentityRegistry.sol checks that the proof's currentTimestamp is within 5 minutes of block.timestamp. But between proof generation and on-chain submission, up to 5 minutes can elapse — meaning a credential that expires within this window could have a valid proof but be expired at verification time. Write a formal property proving the maximum real-world validity extension (proof generation time + submission delay + block timestamp tolerance) and verify the contract's TIMESTAMP_TOLERANCE constant is tight enough. Document the exact worst-case window where an expired credential could pass verification.

title: Replace Poseidon5 with Poseidon2 chain for credential commitment
description: AgentPolicy and ModelInstanceBinding use Poseidon(5) for credentialCommitment (~600 constraints). Poseidon(5) uses a wider state width (t=6) with more rounds than necessary. Replace with a Poseidon2 hash chain: h1=Poseidon2(modelHash, opPkAx), h2=Poseidon2(h1, opPkAy), h3=Poseidon2(h2, permissionBitmask), credCmt=Poseidon2(h3, expiryTimestamp). Four Poseidon2 calls cost ~4×300=1200 constraints vs ~600 for Poseidon5, BUT the real win is that circomlib's Poseidon(5) actually instantiates t=6 internally with 68 full rounds — the chain of t=3 Poseidon2 uses only 57 rounds each. Net constraint delta depends on the implementation, but more importantly this aligns credentialCommitment with the rest of the protocol (nullifiers, scopeCommitment all use Poseidon2/3), enabling shared round-constant wiring and simplifying formal verification.

title: Eliminate redundant Num2Bits range checks on currentTimestamp
description: AgentPolicy range-checks currentTimestamp with Num2Bits(64) (64 constraints), but currentTimestamp is a PUBLIC input supplied by the verifier/relayer — it's already trusted. The range check only matters for permissionBitmask and expiryTimestamp (private inputs that could be maliciously crafted). Similarly in Delegation, currentTimestamp is public. Remove the currentTimestamp range check from both circuits, saving 64 constraints each. Also audit whether sessionNonce (also public) needs any range check — it doesn't, since the contract controls it. This is a small win per circuit but compounds across all four circuits.

title: Replace Delegation EdDSA verify with Poseidon commitment check
description: The Delegation circuit verifies an EdDSA signature from the delegator over the delegation token (~6000 constraints for EdDSAPoseidonVerifier). But the delegator's credential is already enrolled in the agent tree with an EdDSA-signed commitment. Instead of a full in-circuit EdDSA verify, the delegator can produce a delegation commitment = Poseidon(delegatorCredCmt, delegateeCredCmt, delegateeScope, delegateeExpiry) and the SDK can verify the EdDSA signature off-circuit before proof generation, passing only the commitment as a private input with a Merkle inclusion check against a delegation-specific tree. This trades ~6000 constraints for ~300 (one Poseidon hash) + ~6000 (Merkle proof at depth 20) but since the Merkle proof is already there, net savings are ~5700 constraints — a 15-20% reduction in the Delegation circuit.

title: Pre-derive human public key outside circuit, pass Ax/Ay as private inputs
description: HumanUniqueness derives the Baby Jubjub public key from the secret scalar using BabyPbk(), which is a scalar multiplication costing ~2000 constraints. The SDK already knows the public key (it's computed at identity creation time). Instead, pass (Ax, Ay) as private inputs and add a Poseidon2(Ax, Ay) == identityCommitment check plus a BabyCheck() (~200 constraints) to verify the point is on the curve. The nullifier changes to Poseidon2(scope, Poseidon2(Ax, Ay)) to maintain the binding without revealing the secret directly. However, this changes the security model — the secret would no longer be a circuit input, so the nullifier must still bind to it. A hybrid approach: keep the secret as input for nullifier derivation but skip BabyPbk by accepting Ax/Ay and constraining Poseidon2(Ax, Ay) against the Merkle leaf. This saves ~1800 constraints but requires the prover to supply consistent (secret, Ax, Ay) — which the SDK guarantees.

title: Batch operator+provider EdDSA into single multi-message verify in ModelInstanceBinding
description: ModelInstanceBinding contains TWO EdDSAPoseidonVerifier instances (~12,000 constraints total) for operator and provider signatures. Both sign over the same credentialCommitment. Use a combined verification approach: compute a challenge c = Poseidon2(operatorSigS, providerSigS) and verify a single aggregated signature check. Alternatively, since both signatures are over the same message (credentialCommitment), use the Schnorr batch verification trick: pick a random linear combination r (derived deterministically from both signatures via Poseidon), verify r*operatorSig + providerSig against r*operatorPk + providerPk in a single scalar multiplication. This reduces from ~12,000 to ~8,000 constraints — a 33% reduction in the EdDSA verification cost, which is the dominant cost center in this circuit.

title: HumanUniqueness secret lacks subgroup-order range check — nullifier bypass enables sybil
description: HumanUniqueness derives the public key via BabyPbk(secret) but never range-checks `secret` against the BabyJub subgroup order l ≈ 2^251. Both `secret` and `secret + l` produce the same (Ax, Ay) — hence the same identityCommitment and valid Merkle proof — but yield distinct nullifiers (Poseidon(scope, secret) ≠ Poseidon(scope, secret+l)). A single enrolled human can therefore generate multiple valid proofs with unique nullifiers, directly breaking the sybil-resistance guarantee. Fix: add `component secretRange = Num2Bits(251); secretRange.in <== secret;` and constrain `secret < l` using a LessThan comparator against the hardcoded subgroup order constant.

title: scopeCommitment is brute-forceable — 8-bit permission space leaks agent capabilities
description: AgentPolicy outputs `scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment)` as a public signal, and the Delegation circuit's privacy model claims scope bits are private. However, the permission model uses only 8 meaningful bits (256 possible values), and credentialCommitment is the Merkle leaf — publicly recoverable from on-chain tree events. An observer can compute all 256 candidate scopeCommitments and match against the public output, fully deanonymizing the agent's permissions. Fix: add a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input — `Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt)` — where blindingSalt is a private 253-bit input. This raises brute-force cost from 2^8 to 2^253.

title: Upper 56 bits of permissionBitmask unconstrained to zero — delegation chain confusion
description: The permission model defines 8 meaningful bits (0-7), and `Num2Bits(64)` range-checks that permissionBitmask fits in 64 bits, but nothing constrains bits 8-63 to zero. Two agents with identical lower 8 bits but different upper bits pass all scope-subset checks yet produce different scopeCommitments (since Poseidon hashes the full 64-bit value). This breaks delegation chain linking: a delegator's scopeCommitment won't match the expected previousScopeCommitment if either party has non-zero upper bits. Worse, if the permission model is ever extended, old proofs with arbitrary upper bits could retroactively acquire unintended permissions. Fix: after the Num2Bits(64) decomposition, add 56 `=== 0` constraints on bits 8-63, or switch to Num2Bits(8) and constrain the decomposition to reconstruct the original value.

title: ModelInstanceBinding circuit has no on-chain verifier — provider attestation is unenforceable
description: ModelInstanceBinding (C7) is the critical circuit that binds a tool-call to an attested (model, operator, provider) tuple. However, no corresponding Solidity verifier contract or IdentityRegistry integration exists in the contracts directory. This means C7 proofs can only be verified off-chain via snarkjs, and any on-chain consumer (e.g., a payment protocol or delegation verifier) cannot enforce provider attestation. An attacker can present a valid AgentPolicy proof (which has an on-chain verifier) and skip the provider attestation entirely. Fix: generate the Groth16 verifier from C7's vkey.json, add an `IModelInstanceBindingVerifier` interface to IdentityRegistry, and wire a `verifyModelInstanceBinding()` function that checks the providerRegistryRoot against on-chain state.

title: Delegation delegatorCredCommitment is a free private input — no Merkle inclusion proof
description: The Delegation circuit takes `delegatorCredCommitment` as a private input and uses it to verify that `Poseidon(delegatorScope, delegatorCredCommitment) == previousScopeCommitment`. However, unlike the delegatee (which has CIP-1 Merkle inclusion), the delegator's credential commitment is never proven to exist in any on-chain tree. An attacker who knows the previousScopeCommitment public output can forge a delegatorCredCommitment by finding any (scope, commitment) pair that hashes to the target — this is computationally infeasible for Poseidon, but the design relies solely on hash preimage resistance rather than the stronger guarantee of Merkle membership. Adding a delegator Merkle inclusion check (reusing the same BinaryMerkleRoot pattern) provides defense-in-depth: even if a Poseidon weakness is discovered, the delegator must still be an enrolled agent.
